topic: A person discovers the tree they planted as a child has been cut down
raw_output:
[story 1]

The old oak, a towering sentinel in my childhood backyard, was gone. Where its sturdy branches and sprawling canopy once offered shade, there was now only a freshly scarred patch of earth. My breath hitched, a sharp, involuntary gasp catching in my throat. I walked to the edge of the clearing, my hands clenching into fists until my knuckles turned white. The ground felt hollow beneath my worn sneakers. I remembered the painstaking effort of those long summer afternoons, the careful watering, the sheer pride I’d felt watching it grow from a tiny sapling into something magnificent. Now, it was just a vacant space, a stark reminder of something taken. A tightness coiled in my chest, a hot, prickly sensation that made my skin crawl. I stared at the stumps, the raw wood looking like a betrayal.

[story 2]

"That's impossible," I muttered, the sound thin and brittle in the quiet afternoon air. I circled the perimeter of the property, my gaze sweeping over the neighboring yard, searching for any sign of disturbance, any evidence of recent heavy machinery. My jaw was tight, the muscles along my neck corded with tension. It wasn't just a tree; it was a monument to my own earnest efforts, a tangible piece of my younger self. A bitter taste flooded my mouth. I paced back and forth, my movements jerky, as if trying to physically shake the disbelief out of me. Every shadow seemed to stretch and distort, amplifying the feeling that someone else had claimed ownership over something I had nurtured with such devotion.

[story 3]

I knelt by the base of the remaining roots, running my fingers over the damp soil, searching for any lingering trace of life. The silence was oppressive, heavy with unspoken implications. A slow, burning resentment began to bloom in my gut, a strange, unpleasant heat that radiated outward. I remembered the sheer joy of watching that tree flourish, the way its leaves danced in the breeze. Now, the emptiness felt deliberate. I stood up abruptly, pushing my hands through my hair, trying to physically dispel the sudden wave of sourness washing over me. Someone else had gotten to enjoy the view, the shade, the legacy. The injustice of it settled over me like a suffocating blanket.
